description: In a VSF stack, the switch may fail to log events for the matching access-list entries. ACL functionality is not impacted; access-list entries are applied properly and only the logging is incorrectly generated.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what is the caveat related to ACLs in a VSF stack?

Feature Caveat: ACLs - In a VSF stack, the switch may fail to log events for matching access-list entries. ACL functionality is not impacted; access-list entries are applied properly and only the logging is incorrectly generated.

description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Central?

Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

description: When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Certificates?

Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

description: Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Classifiers?
Feature Caveat: Classifiers - Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

description: For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for Classifiers in HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.18.0001?
For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

description: The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?

Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - The Client IP tracker tracks IP addresses until IP source lockdown is applied. After lockdown, only valid binding table entries are tracked. Already tracked IPs remain active through ARP probes, which use Layer 2 packets.

description: No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for CMF?
CMF - No other checkpoint besides 'startup-configuration' gets migrated during the upgrade process.

description: When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Hot Patch?

Feature Caveat: Hot Patch - When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

feature: HPE Aruba Networking CX Mobile App
description: VSF stack formation is blocked when there are reserved autojoin interfaces (25, 26, 49, 50) in the stack topology.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for HPE Aruba Networking CX Mobile App?
Feature Caveat: HPE Aruba Networking CX Mobile App - VSF stack formation is blocked when there are reserved autojoin interfaces (25, 26, 49, 50) in the stack topology.

description: The switch may only forward packets at a rate of 100 packets per second (pps) if they trigger an ICMP redirect.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for ICMP Redirect?
Feature Caveat: ICMP Redirect - The switch may only forward packets at a rate of 100 packets per second (pps) if they trigger an ICMP redirect.

description: Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for IP-SLA?

Feature Caveat: IP-SLA - Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

description: The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for IP Source Lockdown?

Feature Caveat: IP Source Lockdown - The IP source-lockdown feature allows client traffic through the switch only if their MAC and IP addresses are in the binding database. VLAN-level configuration overrides port-level configuration only when the port belongs to a single VLAN; otherwise, VLAN-level configuration is not applied.

description: MACsec works between a CX device and a Windows VM running AnyConnect with AES-128 cipher. AnyConnect does not support AES-256 in the NAM module (works only for the VPN module). NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for HPE Aruba AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - MACsec works between a CX device and a Windows VM running AnyConnect with AES-128 cipher. AnyConnect does not support AES-256 in the NAM module (works only for the VPN module). NOTE MACsec is supported on CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: When using Cisco AnyConnect as a dot1x supplicant, configure the CAK length to 16 under dot1x-authenticator mode. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for AOS-CX 6200 version 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - When using Cisco AnyConnect as a dot1x supplicant, configure the CAK length to 16 under dot1x-authenticator mode. NOTE MACsec is supported on CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: Ensure the cipher suite GCM-AES-128 is configured when AOS-CX is acting as a key server. By default, AOS-CX uses the more secure cipher suite GCM-AES-XPN-256 for establishing MACsec secure links. However, Comware/PVOS does not support the XPN cipher suite.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - Ensure the cipher suite GCM-AES-128 is configured when acting as a key server. By default, AOS-CX uses the more secure cipher suite GCM-AES-XPN-256 for establishing MACsec secure links. However, Comware/PVOS does not support the XPN cipher suite.

description: MACsec uses a software-based implementation to track start and stop times for secure channels and secure associations. As the implementation is software-based, the stop times for MACsec secure channel and secure associations are only updated when they are deleted and therefore never updated in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - The implementation uses a software-based approach to track start and stop times for secure channels and associations. Consequently, the stop times are only updated when the secure channel or association is deleted, and thus are not reflected in the output of the show macsec status detailed command.

description: In an environment with Cisco and FlexFabric or H3C devices, do not update confidentiality-offset on the live channel. There can be complete traffic loss for an extended period on the MACsec channel when confidentiality-offset is updated on both ends. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for AOS-CX 6200 version 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

There can be complete traffic loss for an extended period on the MACsec channel when confidentiality-offset is updated on both ends. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: In an environment with Juniper, configure no include-sci-tag under MACsec policy for data traffic forwarding. NOTE MACsec is supported CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, for AOS-CX 6200 version 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?

Feature Caveat: MACsec - In an environment with Juniper, configure no include-sci-tag under MACsec policy for data traffic forwarding. NOTE MACsec is supported on CX 6200M switch models only (R8Q67A, R8Q68A, R8Q69A, R8Q70A, and R8Q71A, R8V08A, R8V09A, R8V10A, R8V11A, and R8V12A).

description: In an environment with Juniper devices, issue the command no include-sci-tag in a macsec policy context for data traffic forwarding.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for MACsec?
Feature Caveat: MACsec - In an environment with Juniper devices, issue the command no include-sci-tag in a macsec policy context for data traffic forwarding.

description: Passing multicast traffic over GRE, 6in4 or 6in6 tunnels is not supported.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for Multicast?
Feature Caveat: Multicast - Passing multicast traffic over GRE, 6in4 or 6in6 tunnels is not supported.

description: OSPFv2 and OSPFv3 do not support detailed LSA show commands.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what OSPF caveat is documented?
Feature Caveat: OSPF - OSPFv2 and OSPFv3 do not support detailed LSA show commands.

description: Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what RADIUS caveat is documented?
Feature Caveat: RADIUS - Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.

description: REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what is the caveat related to REST?
REST supports 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

description: RIP/RIPng metric configuration support is not available.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for RIP/RIPng?
Feature Caveat: RIP/RIPng - RIP/RIPng metric configuration support is not available.

description: When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what is the caveat related to SNMP?

Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

description: DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for UBT/DHCP-Snooping?
Feature Caveat: UBT/DHCP-Snooping - DHCP Snooping (v4/v6) should not be enabled on the UBT client VLANs in either vlan extend and local vlan mode.

description: Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - Use standalone or cluster-specific IP addresses for primary and backup controllers, ensuring they are not from the same cluster. For the primary controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the primary cluster. For the backup controller use the IP address of a standalone controller or a controller in the backup cluster. Do not configure both primary and backup controller IP addresses from the same cluster.

description: The switch does not support double encapsulation. A packet can be encapsulated with either L2GRE (UBT) or VXLAN, but not both. The network admin should decide the tunneling type to be used, and then plan the configuration accordingly.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - The switch does not support double encapsulation. A packet can be encapsulated with either L2GRE (UBT) or VXLAN, but not both. The network admin should decide the tunneling type to be used, and then plan the configuration accordingly.

description: If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what additional caveat is documented for User Based Tunnel?

Feature Caveat: User Based Tunnel - If there are license issues when onboarding a device to the primary or backup mobility conductor, the device will not switch to another conductor with an available license. If the primary conductor is unreachable, the device will tunnel to the backup conductor. Once the primary conductor is reachable again, the device will not automatically switch back. Administrators must manually disable and enable UBT to re-establish the tunnel to the primary conductor.

description: In 6100(JL679A) and 6200(R8Q72A/R8V13A) switch series, 12-port switches cannot form a stack with 24-port or 48-port switches.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what caveat is documented for VSF?
Feature Caveat: VSF - In 6100(JL679A) and 6200(R8Q72A/R8V13A) switch series, 12-port switches cannot form a stack with 24-port or 48-port switches.

Feature Caveat: VXLAN - Only static VXLAN is supported.

description: Overlay/system partition information is included in HOST-RESOURCES-MIB polling. The overlay partition is a read-only partition and this is reflected in SNMP queries. hrFSAccess.3 = readOnly(2) is expected behavior for overlay partitions. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

messages.content: For 6200 AOS-CX 10.18.0001, what limitation is mentioned for SNMP?

The overlay partition is a read-only partition and this is reflected in SNMP queries. hrFSAccess.3 = readOnly(2) is expected behavior for overlay partitions. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

description: Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header.
workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: CPU usage of ovsdb-server is increased by 9-13% more than expected because ubt sac hearbeat statistics writes into OVSDB. Scenario: This issue may occur when UBT is configured with cluster or multi-zone and enabled in the switch. Then the ovsdb-server process sees an increased CPU usage. Workaround: Configure the SAC heartbeat interval to 2-4 seconds. This decreases the overall ovsdb-server cpu load by 4-5% as the heartbeat write transaction to ovsdb-server decreases corresponding to the configured sac beat interval.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

description: Symptom: On switches running AOS-CX 10.18, a switch that is power-cycled or rebooted within 5 minutes of the login prompt after a zeroize operation, or within 5 minutes of the login prompt after updating to an affected 10.18 build, can encounter a TPM issue on the next boot. The switch may log TPM self-test or measurement errors and may reboot automatically several times before booting with Critical event_ID: 13603 'TPM selftest errors occurred on the current boot'. Scenario: This issue may occur on switches running AOS-CX 10.18 that are power-cycled or rebooted within 5 minutes of the login prompt after a zeroize operation, or within 5 minutes of the login prompt after updating to an affected 10.18 build. Workaround: Reboot or power cycle the switch and wait more than 5 minutes to power cycle or reboot.

description: Symptom: Dual-port AP uplinks connected via a LAG intermittently show member interfaces as “Blocked by Port Access Security.” the LAG stops forwarding and the end device cannot onboard until the interfaces are toggled. Scenario: This issue may occur when port-access device-profile is enabled on the LAG with mode block-until-profile-applied, using LLDP for profiling. When the LAG member that had the LLDP neighbor goes Layer-1 down, subsequent authentication fails and the interface becomes blocked.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected to a switch configured as a DHCP relay agent may fail to obtain an IP address when DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay are both enabled on the same VLAN. DHCP clients that perform explicit source MAC address validation may drop the relayed DHCP response packets. Scenario: This issue occurs when both DHCPv4 Relay and DHCPv4 Snooping are enabled on the same VLAN. In this configuration, DHCP server responses (Offer/Ack) are forwarded to the client with an invalid (all-zeros) source MAC address in the Ethernet header. Workaround: Avoid enabling DHCPv4 Snooping and DHCPv4 Relay simultaneously on the same VLAN.

description: Symptom: EST-based certificate auto-enrollment and re-enrollment fails with an SSL connect error (CURL error 35) on AOS-CX 10.16 and later when the EST server requires TLS client certificate authentication and enforces it via TLS renegotiation. Scenario: This issue may occur when the EST client first retrieves CA certificates over an unauthenticated TLS connection and caches the session. When the server subsequently requests a client certificate by initiating TLS renegotiation on the reused session, the switch rejects the renegotiation, causing enrollment to fail. This behavior is observed with EST servers such as EJBCA when configured to require mutual TLS (mTLS) client certificate authentication. Workaround: Configure the EST server to accept password-based authentication (HTTP Digest or Basic) as an alternative to requiring TLS client certificate authentication on the enrollment endpoint, and configure username and password credentials in the AOS-CX EST profile. This changes the authentication method for EST enrollment.

description: Symptom: When ip forward-protocol udp is configured with a non-classful subnet broadcast address as the destination (for example, 10.232.104.127 for a /25 subnet), the forwarded packet is sent as unicast to that address instead of being broadcast on the destination subnet. Hosts on the destination subnet do not receive the forwarded UDP broadcast. Scenario: This issue occurs when the destination address configured in ip forward-protocol udp is a non-classful subnet broadcast address (not ending in.255). The UDP broadcast forwarder only recognizes classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as broadcast destinations. Any other address, including valid subnet broadcast addresses for prefix lengths shorter than /24 (such as /25, /26, etc.), is treated as a unicast destination. The switch identifies a configured address as a broadcast destination solely based on whether the last octet is 255, regardless of the subnet prefix length. Workaround: Use only classful subnet broadcast addresses (ending in.255) as destinations in ip forward-protocol udp when broadcast delivery on the destination subnet is required. For non-/24 subnets where the broadcast address does not end in.255, configure each unicast destination host IP individually. A maximum of 8 forward-protocol entries are supported per interface.

description: Symptom: CPU usage of ovsdb-server is increased by 9-13% more than expected because ubt sac hearbeat statistics writes into OVSDB. Scenario: This issue may occur when UBT is configured with cluster or multi-zone and enabled in the switch. Then the ovsdb-server process sees an increased CPU usage. Workaround: Configure the SAC heartbeat interval to 2-4 seconds. This decreases the overall ovsdb-server cpu load by 4-5% as the heartbeat write transaction to ovsdb-server decreases corresponding to the configured sac beat interval.

workaround: Configure the SAC heartbeat interval to 2-4 seconds. This decreases the overall ovsdb-server cpu load by 4-5% as the heartbeat write transaction to ovsdb-server decreases corresponding to the configured sac beat interval.

description: Symptom: DHCP clients connected via a relay agent either fail to obtain an IP address or receive an IP address from an incorrect DHCP pool instead of the pool intended for their subnet. Scenario: This issue occurs when the DHCP server has multiple pools configured with IP ranges that span non-default subnet boundaries (e.g., /23), and the prefix-len parameter is not explicitly specified in the range command. When a relay agent forwards client DISCOVER packets, the DHCP server uses the relay agent's gateway IP address (giaddr) to identify the matching pool. Without prefix-len, the server cannot determine the pool's subnet boundary and fails to correctly map the giaddr to the intended pool. Workaround: Explicitly specify prefix-len in the pool range configuration to match the intended subnet mask. For example: dhcp-server vrf default pool pool_name_ABC range 172.22.0.4 172.22.1.254 prefix-len <netmask>.

description: Symptom: On switches running AOS-CX 10.18, a switch that is power-cycled or rebooted within 5 minutes of the login prompt after a zeroize operation, or within 5 minutes of the login prompt after updating to an affected 10.18 build, can encounter a TPM issue on the next boot. The switch may log TPM self-test or measurement errors and may reboot automatically several times before booting with Critical event_ID: 13603 'TPM selftest errors occurred on the current boot'. Scenario: This issue may occur on switches running AOS-CX 10.18 that are power-cycled or rebooted within 5 minutes of the login prompt after a zeroize operation, or within 5 minutes of the login prompt after updating to an affected 10.18 build. Workaround: Reboot or power cycle the switch and wait more than 5 minutes to power cycle or reboot.
